title: McGill Financial Asset Management Hackathon '25
summary: QUARCC placed 4th at Mcgill FIAM 2025. By Theodor Semerdzhiev, Carter Cameron, and Luyun Chen
detail:
$3,000 Team Prize; 4th place. 

Over more than two months, our team designed a trading strategy built around a coordinated ensemble, not just a collection of models, but a system where each component contributed a unique perspective on expected returns. 

At its foundation were specialized sub-models, each tuned to capture a different dimension of market behavior. Our text-based models analyzed management discussions to quantify risk tone and surprise sentiment, drawing on fine-tuned regional LLMs to avoid the look-ahead bias that plagues newer financial NLP models like FINBERT. On the quantitative side, a regime-clustering model helped identify prevailing market conditions, which then interacted with factor-momentum features to detect shifts in style leadership. Complementing this, a vector similarity search model compared today’s environment to historical market patterns, asking in effect, “When have we seen this before, and what happened next?” These insights converged in a meta-model that didn’t just average predictions; it learned how to listen. Composed of four dynamically weighted learners, it adapted its weighting scheme based on recent validation performance, ensuring that the models most attuned to current conditions had the greatest voice. This architecture powered our focus on high-potential small-cap stocks, yielding one of the strongest risk-adjusted returns in the competition. We paired the predictive core with a realistic investment framework incorporating leverage, portfolio weighting, and transaction costs, turning predictions into a disciplined, executable portfolio.

Coming from computer science and engineering backgrounds, we placed a strong emphasis on robustness, out-of-sample reliability, and clean data flows. In hindsight, our approach may have been more rigorous than rhetorical. The experience underscored that at the intersection of computer science and finance, outcomes hinge not only on modeling strength but also on the clarity of the narrative behind it. 

Thanks to our assigned mentor Frank Leclerc for two days of invaluable guidance on portfolio construction and pitching, and to Theodor Semerdzhiev, Carter Cameron, and Luyun Chen for their contributions throughout the process. A final sincere thanks to Russ Goyenko and the organizing team for running such a well-executed and engaging competition.

We are proud to have finished strong among a competitive field and to have grown through the experience. On to the next one, with both the math and the message in mind.
